mcqs.question: What is the primary purpose of unification in the context of predicate logic resolution?

options.text: To prove that two literals can be derived from each other
To find a substitution that makes two atomic formulas identical
To convert sentences to clause form for resolution
To eliminate existential quantifiers through Skolemization

mcqs.explanation: Unification is the process of finding a substitution that makes two atomic formulas (literals) identical by replacing variables with terms.

mcqs.question: What does the resolution principle achieve in automated theorem proving?

options.text: It converts CNF to DNF for simplification
It is a refutation-complete inference rule that derives new clauses from two parent clauses containing complementary literals
It applies substitutions to clauses to simplify them
It checks satisfiability of Horn clauses only

mcqs.explanation: Resolution is a refutation-complete inference rule used in automated theorem proving that works on clauses in conjunctive normal form by resolving on complementary literals.

mcqs.question: What is a Most General Unifier (MGU)?

options.text: A unification algorithm that always terminates in O(n) time
A substitution that makes two terms identical and is less restrictive than any other unifier for the same pair of terms
A unification algorithm used only in propositional logic
A substitution that replaces all variables with constant terms

mcqs.explanation: An MGU is a unifier that makes two expressions identical while being most general, meaning any other unifier can be obtained by composing it with additional substitutions.

mcqs.question: Consider the formula: ∃x ∀y (P(x,y) → Q(y)). What is the clause form after Skolemization?

options.text: ¬P(x,y) ∨ Q(y)
¬P(a,y) ∨ Q(y) where a is a Skolem constant
P(x,f(x)) → Q(f(x))

mcqs.explanation: Skolemization removes the existential quantifier by introducing a Skolem constant 'a' for x, resulting in the clause ¬P(a,y) ∨ Q(y).

mcqs.question: In the Set of Support (SOS) resolution strategy, what is the constraint on resolution steps?

options.text: At least one parent clause must be a unit clause
At least one parent clause must come from the negated goal (the set of support)
Resolution can only be performed on input clauses, not derived ones
All parent clauses must contain only positive literals

mcqs.explanation: The Set of Support strategy requires at least one parent clause in each resolution step to come from the negated goal, ensuring the search is directed toward proving the goal.

mcqs.question: Given substitutions θ = {x/f(y), z/a} and σ = {y/g(x), x/b}, what is the composition θσ?

options.text: {x/b, y/g(f(y)), z/a}
{x/b, y/g(b), z/a}
{x/f(g(x)), y/g(x), z/a}
{x/b, y/g(b), x/f(y), z/a}

mcqs.explanation: In composition θσ, we apply σ to the terms in θ first, then add bindings from σ not in θ. Applying σ gives {x/b, y/g(x), z/a}, and σ adds {y/g(b)} giving {x/b, y/g(b), z/a}.

mcqs.question: Given clauses: C1: P(x, f(x)) and C2: ¬P(a, y) ∨ Q(y), what is the resolvent after applying resolution with proper unification?

options.text: Q(f(a))
Q(a)
P(a, f(a))

mcqs.explanation: Unifying P(x, f(x)) with ¬P(a, y) gives substitution {x/a, y/f(a)}. Applying this to Q(y) produces the resolvent Q(f(a)).

mcqs.question: Why does the unification algorithm fail (returns 'failure') when attempting to unify P(x) with P(f(x))?

options.text: Because f(x) is a function symbol and x is a variable
Because the occurs check fails: x appears within f(x), which would create an infinite term f(f(f(...))) if allowed
Because the algorithm has a time complexity issue with this case
Because the predicate symbols do not match

mcqs.explanation: The occurs check prevents unifying a variable with a term containing that variable, as it would create a cyclic/infinite structure that cannot be represented finitely.
